DDS

Data-Driven Strategy

Research drives the strategy.

Every recommendation is labeled KEEP, REVISE, TEST, or REMOVE. The purpose of DDS is to challenge our assumptions and improve the campaign—not to justify decisions already made.

Message Design

Evidence-Based

Use personal contact

DDS decision: KEEP. Personal, conversational contact generally performs better than impersonal campaign contact. This supports writing as one named volunteer rather than as an organization.

Read the Yale evidence ↗
Needs Testing

A/B/C/D version text testing

DDS decision: TEST. Research does not identify one universally best opening, so Text 1 runs four versions head to head: A Friendly Neighbor, B Transparent Authority, C Shared Mission, and D Urgent Appeal. The version chosen carries through Texts 2-5, and we track which drives the most responses.

Why randomized testing matters ↗
Needs Testing

Urgency and "we need you" appeals

DDS decision: TEST. Social-pressure and urgency framing can lift turnout more than a plain reminder in the field-experiment record, but message content matters less than personalization and timing, and heavy guilt-based pressure risks backlash and the group's reputation. Practitioners favor positive, deadline-driven urgency over shame. One reliable, peer-reviewed anchor: prompting a concrete plan to vote right before an imminent election ("when, where, how") raised turnout 4.1 points in a 287,000-person field experiment (Nickerson & Rogers, 2010) — evidence for pairing urgency with a clear next step, not pressure alone. This is why the urgent version keeps a deadline and a one-tap "reply L" action.

Nickerson & Rogers 2010 (Psychological Science) ↗ Donald Green on social pressure ↗

Texting Strategy

Evidence-Based

Texts can produce modest gains

DDS decision: KEEP, but measure. Very large field experiments show that simple text reminders can create small but measurable effects. Texting should be treated as a useful nudge, not a guaranteed solution.

Read the field experiment ↗
Needs Testing

Exact cadence and number of texts

DDS decision: TEST. Public research does not establish that five texts, our spacing, or our chosen send times are optimal. Track response and STOP rates by send.

Read the textbanking research summary ↗

Personal Connection

Campaign Field Test

Trusted messengers matter

DDS decision: KEEP and strengthen. Conservative field testing supports relational contact and trusted messengers. Our first-person Vincent voice aligns with this direction.

Read the conservative field test ↗
Needs Testing

Phone calls after three unanswered texts

DDS decision: TEST. Phone contact can work, but results vary with call quality and context. “After three texts” is an operational hypothesis, not a proven threshold.

Read the phonebanking research summary ↗
Evidence-Based

Phone banking: quality beats volume

DDS decision: KEEP these principles; TEST the registration use. In randomized field experiments, volunteer calls that are personal and unhurried raised turnout about 3.8 points, at a cost per added vote competitive with door knocking (Nickerson, 2006). Rushed, scripted commercial calls and robocalls show little to no effect. What the evidence supports:

• Real volunteers, not robocalls or paid rooms. • A warm, conversational call, not a rote script. • Part of a timed, multi-touch sequence, coordinated with the texts, not a flood. • Help them make a concrete plan and act on the spot. Note: this evidence is about turnout; using a call to follow up on registration after Text 3 is promising but unproven, so treat our after-Text-3 call as a TEST.

Nickerson 2006, Volunteer Phone Calls Can Increase Turnout ↗

Measurement

Evidence-Based Method

A/B/C/D testing

DDS decision: KEEP. Random assignment is necessary to distinguish a message effect from differences in the people receiving each version. With four versions (A/B/C/D), give each a fair share of sends so the comparison stays valid.

Read why randomization matters ↗

Project Evaluation

Evaluate

Texting Platform Evaluation

  • Which of the four first text versions (A/B/C/D) performed better?
    • Reply rate by version, but never pick a "winner" on reply rate alone.
  • Link requests ("L") and mail requests ("M") per version.
  • Completed voter registrations where measurable, tied back to version (the real conversion).
  • Opt-out ("STOP") rate by version and by send.
  • Wrong-number and undeliverable rate (list quality and deliverability).
  • Best send day, time, and cadence across the five-text sequence.
  • How many people registered after the August 22 phone call?
  • Make sure we can track who voted on November 3rd, then keep following and re-engaging them through subsequent elections to measure whether they continue to vote.
  • Cost per completed registration.
PHONE CALL FOLLOW-UP

The After-Text-3 Call

A live volunteer call, placed after Text 3 to contacts who still have not registered.

The evidence rewards a real conversation over a rote read: warm, unhurried volunteer calls move people, while rushed paid calls and robocalls do little. Using a call to follow up on registration rather than turnout is promising but unproven, so this is a TEST. Script #3, Shared Mission, was chosen because a genuine chat outperforms a script read aloud.

The Call (Shared Mission)

Opener

Hi (insert resident's name), it's Vincent, a volunteer in Bucks County. I'll be quick - I've been reaching out because races around here get decided by just a handful of votes, and I'd hate for your voice to be left off the rolls. Have you had a chance to register yet?

If they are not registered

Let's fix that today then. I can send you the official state link right now - three minutes, and it makes sure your conservative vote actually counts on November 3rd. The deadline to be registered is October 19th. Can I text it over?

If yes: text the registration link on the spot, then confirm they received it before you hang up.

If they are hesitant

I hear you. No pressure at all - but the door closes October 19th, so if it matters to you even a little, now's the easy time. Want the link just in case?

Voicemail

Hi (insert resident's name), Vincent here, Bucks County volunteer. Your vote matters and the registration deadline is October 19th - I'll text you the official link so it's easy. Thanks, and hope to help you get it done.

If They Sound Wary

Fallback line (only if needed)

I'm a volunteer, not a salesman - I just help folks get registered.

Do not open the call with "I'm not selling anything." It plants the frame and sounds scripted. Use the line above only if a contact already sounds guarded.

How To Run It

TEST
  • Have a real conversation. Let them talk; do not read straight through.
  • Stay warm and unhurried. Quality beats volume on the phone.
  • If you reach them and they are not registered, text the link during the call and help them start it right then.
  • Use the fallback line only if they sound wary, never as your opener.
  • No answer? Leave the voicemail, then let the text sequence continue.

Placed after Text 3 in the send schedule, this call is one more personal touch, not a flood. Track how many calls connect and how many lead to a completed registration.
